title: Inner Word vs Around Word
subtitle: {key:iw} and {key:aw} — the first text objects.
summary: Text objects produce a (start, end) range *without* using the cursor. iw is the word the cursor is in. aw is that word plus surrounding whitespace.

blocks.text: Up to now, the noun in operator+motion has been a *motion* — a path from where you are to where you'll end up. **Text objects** change that. They name a chunk of text directly, no path required. The first one to learn is the word.

blocks.label: Delete the word the cursor is on (no spaces)
Delete the word AND the surrounding space
Yank the word and a space

blocks.caption: iw vs aw — inner vs around

blocks.title: i Means "Inner." a Means "Around."
blocks.text:
Every text object comes in two flavors:

  • {key:i}{noun} — **inner**: the noun itself, *without* delimiters or surrounding whitespace.
  • {key:a}{noun} — **around**: the noun *with* its delimiters or trailing whitespace.

For a word ({key:i}{key:w} / {key:a}{key:w}), there are no delimiters — just whitespace. So:

  • {key:i}{key:w} = the keyword characters under the cursor.
  • {key:a}{key:w} = the word + the trailing space (or leading space if at end of line).

For a quoted string, {key:i}{key:"} is what's between the quotes; {key:a}{key:"} includes the quotes. For parens, {key:i}{key:(} is the contents; {key:a}{key:(} includes the parens themselves. Same `i`/`a` rule everywhere.

blocks.title: Why Text Objects Aren't Motions
blocks.text:
A motion takes you from the cursor *to* somewhere else. So {key:d}{key:w} deletes from the cursor *forward* to the start of the next word. The cursor's position matters.

A text object is **cursor-position-tolerant**: if the cursor is anywhere inside the chunk, the chunk is the chunk. {key:d}{key:i}{key:w} on any character of `foo` deletes `foo`. You don't need to be at the start.

This is why text objects are easier on your fingers than motions for in-place edits. Combined with {key:c}{key:i}{key:w} for "change-this-word-no-matter-where-the-cursor-is," they're the workhorse of Vim editing.

Word objects use the same `'iskeyword'` setting as {key:w}. So in `foo->bar`, {key:c}{key:i}{key:w} on `foo` changes just `foo`. WORD objects ({key:i}{key:W}, {key:a}{key:W}) treat anything-not-whitespace as the word — change the whole `foo->bar`.
